27CH-GNX: Born in colonial Rhode Island, Jacob Davis eventually made his home in Virginia, where he answered the call of the Revolution. Records of his military service in late 1776 and again in 1778 mark him as one of the patriots who took up arms during the war for independence. He died in Nottoway County, Virginia, in 1794.
9J7J-3GF: A young surgeon trained by the Order of Malta, Jacques Bourgeois sailed from La Rochelle in 1642, arriving at Port-Royal among eighteen families brought to Acadia for a fresh start. He served as a lieutenant and surgeon, was granted his own island in the Annapolis River, and in 1671 founded the settlement of Beaubassin, where he built flour and saw mills and lived out his days as a respected community leader.
9WD5-LBY: Elias Chaffin's long life traced the young nation's westward push, from his birth in Pennsylvania through homes in North Carolina and Kentucky. He served his country during the War of 1812 and continued in military service into the 1830s. Settling at last in Clinton, Illinois, he died there in 1837 after decades of frontier migration.
G9LW-LVW: Thomas Ray left Lincolnshire, England, for the New World, arriving at Jamestown in 1636 and securing a land grant in Virginia by 1642. He raised a large family of sons and daughters along the Charles River. He set down his will in August 1654 and died that same month in York County, having helped plant his family firmly in colonial Virginia soil.
K89V-CMV: Dr. James Spier was a physician of the early colonial South, born in Nansemond County, Virginia, in 1673. By 1704 he held two hundred acres there before moving south into North Carolina. He died in Bertie County in 1731, leaving behind the modest documentary traces of a frontier doctor's life.
KLW3-9VJ: Captain Benjamin Bridges was born in 1664 in Topsfield, within the Massachusetts Bay Colony, and lived his life in colonial New England. He died in Framingham in 1725. Though little documentation of his story survives, his title of captain hints at standing in his community.
KZ28-LRY: Baptized in the Buckinghamshire village of Sherington in 1624, Susanna Newhall was of sturdy Puritan stock. She and her husband Richard Haven emigrated to America as newlyweds around 1645, arriving not as indentured servants but as freemen, and settled in Lynn, Massachusetts. There she helped found a Haven family line that would call Massachusetts home for six generations.
L29V-WHF: Born in Bucks County, Pennsylvania, in 1745, Thomas Lamb served as a soldier of the Revolution, with documented service in 1781 and 1782. After the war he migrated south to the Tennessee frontier, settling in Williamson County. He and his wife Katherine raised a large family and were laid to rest in the Lamb Cemetery near Versailles Knob.
L45Q-W22: Elizabeth Hext was born in 1723 in Charleston, South Carolina, into the colonial Lowcountry. Records note her connection to military service in 1755, during the unsettled years of the colonial frontier wars. She lived her life in South Carolina and died in 1769.
L5NS-M11: John Combs was a Virginia planter, born and died in the colony along the rivers of the Tidewater. From his lands in Hanover Parish, Richmond County, he provided for a wife, Hannah, and six children, including a youngest son who inherited two hundred acres in Essex County. His will, signed in December 1716 as he lay sick and weak, was proved early the following year.
L7GG-68R: James Thelaball carried the Huguenot faith of his French Protestant roots across the Atlantic to Virginia. By 1659 he served as Church Warden of the Elizabeth River Parish in Norfolk County, a sign of his standing among his neighbors. He lived in Lynnhaven Parish until his death in 1693, his will recorded that September.
LB7N-J3Q: Agnes Crockett was born in County Antrim in 1714, part of the Scots-Irish migration that reshaped colonial America. She crossed the ocean in 1740, arriving at Philadelphia, and made her way through Pennsylvania and Virginia before settling in the South Carolina backcountry. She died in 1770, her burial recorded at Glebe Cemetery in Augusta County, Virginia.
LBY4-C5N: Nicholas Gentry came to Virginia from England around 1675 as an indentured servant, following his older brother Samuel to the colony. After working off his term, he became a tenant farming 250 acres of Crown land in New Kent County, which he held for two decades. The immigrant ancestor of a large Gentry line, he died in 1709, leaving sons and daughters baptized at St. Peter's Parish.
LCWK-21J: Nicholas Outhouse was born in Queens, New York, in 1726, and his fortunes turned with the Revolution. As a United Empire Loyalist faithful to the Crown, he left his New York home and resettled in Nova Scotia by 1790, later moving on to Upper Canada. He died at Charlotteville in Ontario in 1804, far from where his life began.
LDMY-5LD: John Lamont Young descended from a Scottish clan whose refugees, fleeing the Toward Castle massacre, changed their surname to find safety. An Ulster Scot who lived for a time in County Antrim, Ireland, he married in Dublin in 1694 and crossed to New York around 1710. He eventually settled in Augusta County, Virginia, where he is said to have lived to a remarkable old age.
LHLP-Y7L: Letitia Thompson was born in 1753 in Guilford County, North Carolina, the daughter of Reverend Robert Thompson, remembered as an early martyr of the cause of American independence. Her family's patriot ties ran deep through the Revolution. She later moved to Williamson County, Tennessee, and became the mother of Newton Cannon, dying in 1830.
LJYD-THT: Colonel John Catlett left Kent, England, and immigrated to Virginia in 1650, where he rose to remarkable prominence. He patented several thousand acres along the Rappahannock, worked as an expert surveyor, and served as presiding justice, burgess, sheriff, and colonel of the militia, even helping settle the Virginia–Maryland boundary line. He was killed in 1670 defending Port Royal during an Indian attack.
LKYD-46R: Benjamin FitzRandolph II was born in 1699 in Piscataway, in colonial New Jersey, where his family had long been established. He held the military rank of ensign in Middlesex County. He died in Princeton in 1758, having spent his life within the colony of his birth.
LT2W-Y4Y: Lieutenant John Ham was born in 1649 in Dover, New Hampshire, one of the colony's early settlements, and lived his whole life there until his death in 1727. Though little of his story survives in record, his rank of lieutenant suggests a man trusted in the defense of his frontier town.
LTZX-SMW: Harold Joseph Lagroue Sr. was a lifelong son of New Orleans, born there in 1893 and raised in the city's Orleans Parish. He registered for the military draft during the First World War. A United States citizen, he lived in New Orleans through the decades, his passing noted in a 1976 obituary in the city he never left.
LWPX-RQG: William Thornton II was born in colonial Virginia in 1649 and spent his life accumulating and trading land across the Rappahannock country, with deeds and residences recorded from Old Rappahannock to Stafford and King George counties. The Thornton name carried standing in Virginia for generations. He drew up his will in 1726, and it was proved that October.
LY5X-LMX: Reuben Middlemiss Chisolm was born in Louisiana in 1873 and spent his childhood for a time in Fort Worth, Texas, before the family returned to New Orleans. He lived out his adult years in the city, faithfully registering to vote through several elections from 1908 onward. He died in New Orleans in 1930.
LZ1Y-1DH: Matthew Barnes Sr. was born in 1670 at Port Tobacco, in colonial Maryland, and lived his entire life in Charles County. A planter of property in tobacco and land, he provided in his will for his wife Mary, his sons, and four daughters. He is also remembered for a connection to Revolutionary War service; his estate was settled in 1746.
LZD9-C3R: John Combes was among the earliest English arrivals to Virginia, sailing aboard the ship Marigold and docking at Jamestown on 20 May 1619, a full year before the Mayflower reached Plymouth. He came as an indentured servant seeking a new life. His son Archdale, born at Jamestown in 1625, would carry the Combs name forward into the Virginia frontier.
LZKN-5WC: Joseph Treat was born in 1662 in Milford, within the Connecticut Colony, and lived his life in that New England town. He carried the rank of captain, a mark of leadership among his neighbors. He died in Milford in 1721 and rests in the town's old cemetery.
M851-RCD: Daniel Gaines came from Aber-Brân in Breconshire, Wales, arriving in Virginia around 1650, where he married the widow Margaret Bernard Rowzee. A captain in the colonial militia, he patented four hundred acres along the Rappahannock and became the first Justice of the Peace in Rappahannock County, remembered as a distinguished and trusted man. He wrote his will in 1682 and died two years later.
MPJB-N33: John Lamp Kouns was born in Virginia in 1780 and lived a life of steady westward movement across the early frontier. He settled for three decades in Greenup County, Kentucky, before moving on to Boone County, Indiana, by 1840. He died at Rosston, Indiana, in 1851, having helped carry his family into the new states of the Ohio Valley.
